text: In the last year of his lifetime, the old thinker, an upright and humane man, settled into a quiet residence near a field where a plough still turned the earth. His health was failing, and a profound anxiety, almost a jealousy of younger minds, would sometimes madden him. Yet he taught to the very end, since teaching gave him pleasure that nothing else could match. "Everybody must defend the liberty to think," he would say, gazing through the window at a swan on the pond, a horse at the fence, a bird in the granary eaves. His style of speech was hearty and genuine, never plausible nonsense dressed as wisdom. Each session, he would dispense one idea, perfect in its symmetry, and let his students sharpen it. He had no weapon but reason; revenge and political faction held nothing for him. He spoke of an episode from his youth, when a cruel accident had nearly killed him. A craft he sailed had struck gravel near the shore, and only the genuine courage of a fellow passenger had saved him. "Such things," he said, "teach you that nowhere is safe, and that we hold our possession of life on loan." The memory still made him gaze into the fireplace, evidently moved. His ardent students would not let his thought perish. They consume his every word, eager to obtain a manuscript he had long postponed publishing. An Australian scholar arrived to appoint herself his editor; respectively, others took on the index, the notes, the specification of each footnote. The likelihood of finishing in time was small, but they refused to be mistaken about their duty. The thinker, shrewd to the last, would tread carefully around any flaw in his old logic. "Do not segregate the heart from the mind," he warned. "To exterminate feeling is to ruin thought." He had once seen a colony of swans, white against a grey sky, and the marvellous sight had become a private weapon against despair. Now, in his rusty old age, he returned to it often. One six-line poem he had written, fluorescent in its clarity, spoke of a god of small mercies and of the sacrifice that any worthy life demands. He would not boast of it. As October cooled the air, an actor friend came to read the verse aloud, and a withdrawal of sorrow seemed to follow. The thinker felt, for once, no inconvenience, no commonsense fear. He had done his work satisfactorily, actively, and with merchandise of the soul that no liquor or rusty grief could spoil. "I have nothing left to prove," he said, his liberty intact. Outside, the swan swam on; the bird sang; and the old man, content at last, watched the year turn. His neighbor, a practical farmer, once joked that it was ludicrous to fling pesticide on a healthy crop; prevention, he said, was exactly the better path. The outturn of the harvest, recorded module by module on a sheet of cardboard, exceeded every estimate. As chairman of the local organization, the thinker had an obligation he would never postpone: to teach grammar, even the humble transitive verb, to the village children. They would swim in summer, play volleyball, go camping, and chop onion for the campfire stew. When a tyre went flat or a leakage of rain broke the deadlock of a dull afternoon, he simply smiled.
translation: 在他一生的最后一年里，这位年迈的思想家——一个正直而仁慈的人——在一片仍有犁翻土的田野附近的安静住所安顿下来。他的健康每况愈下，一种深沉的忧虑，近乎对年轻头脑的嫉妒，有时会让他发狂。然而他直到最后仍在教学，因为教学给他一种别的任何东西都无法比拟的快乐。"每个人都必须捍卫思考的自由，"他会说，透过窗户凝望池塘上的一只天鹅、栅栏边的一匹马、谷仓檐下的一只鸟。他说话的风格热忱而真诚，绝非披着智慧外衣的似是而非的胡言。每一堂课，他都会施予一个观念，其对称之美完美无瑕，并让学生去打磨它。除了理性，他没有别的武器；复仇与政治派系于他毫无意义。他讲起年轻时的一段往事，当时一场残酷的意外几乎要了他的命。他驾驶的一艘小船在岸边撞上碎石，全靠同船一位旅客真正的勇气才救了他。"这种事，"他说，"教你明白没有任何地方是安全的，我们对生命的拥有不过是借来的。"这段记忆仍让他凝望着壁炉，显然为之动容。他那些热心的学生不会让他的思想消亡。他们吞咽他的每一个字，急切地想得到一份他长久推迟出版的手稿。一位澳大利亚学者前来，自荐当他的编辑；其余的人则分别承担索引、注释、每条脚注的详述。按时完成的可能性很小，但他们拒绝在自己的职责上搞错。这位思想家精明到最后，会小心地绕开他旧逻辑中的任何瑕疵。"不要把心与脑隔离开，"他告诫，"消灭情感就是毁掉思想。"他曾见过一群天鹅，在灰色天空映衬下洁白如雪，那绝妙的景象成了他对抗绝望的私人武器。如今，在他生锈般的暮年，他常常重温它。他写过一首六行的诗，清澈得如同荧光，讲述一位主管细小慈悲的神，以及任何值得过的人生所要求的牺牲。他不会以此自夸。当十月使空气转凉，一位演员朋友前来朗读这首诗，悲伤的退潮似乎随之而来。思想家这一次感到没有不便，没有出于常识的恐惧。他已圆满地、积极地完成了自己的工作，带着任何酒或生锈的悲伤都无法败坏的灵魂的货品。"我再没有什么需要证明的了，"他说，自由完好无损。屋外，天鹅继续游着；鸟儿歌唱；这位老人终于心满意足，看着这一年流转。他的邻居是个务实的农民，曾打趣说，往健康的庄稼上抛洒杀虫剂是荒唐的；他说，预防恰恰是更好的路。收成的卸货情况一格一格地记在一张硬纸板上，超出了所有的估计。作为当地组织的主席，这位思想家有一项他从不推迟的义务：教村里的孩子语法，连那不起眼的及物动词也教。他们夏天游泳，打排球，去野营，为篝火炖菜切洋葱。当一只轮胎瘪了，或一场雨的渗漏打破了一个沉闷下午的僵局，他只是微笑。
